title: Jump Game
problem_statement: You are given an integer array nums. You are initially positioned at the array's first index, and each element in the array represents your maximum jump length at that position. Return true if you can reach the last index, or false otherwise.

examples.explanation: Jump 1 step from index 0 to 1, then 3 steps to the last index.
You will always arrive at index 3 no matter what. Its maximum jump length is 0, which makes it impossible to reach the last index.

intuition: We need to determine if there's a path from index 0 to n-1 where each jump stays within bounds and respects the jump lengths.

approaches.name: Recursive Brute Force
approaches.description: From each position, try all possible jumps (1 to nums[i]). Recurse if you can reach further. Base: if current >= n-1, true.
approaches.why_not: Exponential due to exploring all jump paths. Overlapping subproblems make it inefficient for large n.
interview_commentary: This is like a graph where each index has edges to index + 1 to index + nums[i]. We need to see if there's a path to n-1.
The recursion tries every possible jump from each position, which can lead to exponential time if there are many choices.
Many subproblems are recomputed, like whether we can reach from index 5.

approaches.name: Memoized Recursion (Top-Down DP)
approaches.description: Same recursion, but memoize whether each index can reach the end. Use a boolean array or map.
approaches.why_not: Still O(n^2) in worst case (e.g., nums[i]=n for all), but greedy is O(n) and optimal.
interview_commentary: To optimize, I'll memoize on the index: can we reach the end from here?
This avoids recomputing for the same index. Time becomes O(sum of nums[i]) which is O(n^2) worst case.
Space is O(n) for the memo array.

approaches.name: Greedy Reachability
approaches.description: Keep track of the farthest index reachable so far. If at any point current index > farthest, cannot reach. Update farthest with max(farthest, i + nums[i]).
approaches.why_not: This is the most efficient solution, better than DP for this problem.
interview_commentary: Greedy works because we only care about the maximum reach. As we go, update the farthest we can go.
If we ever get stuck (i > reach), we can't proceed. Otherwise, we can reach the end.
This is O(n) time and O(1) space, much better than DP.

interview_qa.a: Because the problem only asks if it's possible, not the path. The farthest reachable index captures all necessary information.
If a 0 blocks the path (i.e., reach doesn't go beyond it), then false. Otherwise, you can jump over it.

interview_qa.q: How does this differ from Jump Game II (minimum jumps)?
interview_qa.a: Jump Game II counts the minimum number of jumps, requiring DP or BFS to track jump counts.

interview_qa.q: Is there a case where DP is needed?
interview_qa.a: For Jump Game II, yes, but for this (reachability), greedy suffices.
